speaker: interviewee
text: No. No nuclear-- and it's a very peaceful country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Six and--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling. So you can ID it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Should I say it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: July-- excuse me. This is July 7, 1995. We are interviewing Magda Mautner Marx. And this is tape six.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] was a-- was-- he proposed to me. But I didn't know him only for a month before that. But in the meantime, he got very friendly with my son and my daughter-in-law. And I called him. And I told him I will come to America. And we will get a little more know each other. And I can not-- I think this was not enough time to know each other to get married.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I really came. And I liked him. He was very nice, very intelligent. He was one and a half years widowed. And so he had a business in educational toy and school, a big man. And was the vice president in B'nai B'rith. And was very intellectual. So we understood each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in November 18, 1970, we got married. In the meantime, my son got already married. And it was very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my son finished the first year in the university in Jerusalem-- I think I mentioned that-- and then came to America, got a scholarship in Georgetown University. So he got his second, I think, masters degree. And then he was working in a, in a medical laboratory room. And from that he was invited-- or I don't know how he got to the Rockefeller University where they take only the top of the top of the students, because that is a hundred students to 1,000 professors. So he was working in mass spectrometry. I think it is a very difficult word. And he became-- he got his PhD there and then became associate professor. And then he saw that he wanted to have a government job, because he got already three children. And the salary was not great. So, he was invited to the National Bureau of Standards in Maryland. And he worked there. And he is a chemist, research chemist.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the meantime, he got involved in the anti-nuclear movement. He had a lot of fear in him because he was-- from the minute that he was born to now, he always went through different type of fear and excitement. So in this movement, he saw somehow from inside that it is not-- that there is a real danger that can happen in a nuclear war-- at that time was still the Cold War.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was sent to all over the world to conferences in his field and was sent once to Australia and New Zealand. And he fell in love with New Zealand. It is peaceful. It's beautiful. Is no nuclear whatsoever. But there is not a lot of possibilities financially because is only three and a half million people live there. But anyhow, my daughter-in-law got a job. And they were very happy there and still are. So my son-in-law gets a contract in several universities here in the United States. And he has to come at least once or twice a year to make his research, what  he works out then. He works part-time in the university in, in Christchurch in New Zealand. So in the meantime, my husband-- we were married two and a half years. And he was already 70, 76 and was very tired. And I convinced him luckily to sell his business. And we, we booked a tour. And he got a attack in Italy, in Murano.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we came back from that. He was four weeks in the hospital. And we came back, and the next week he fell and broke his thigh bone. And then he got one, one heart failure, and the other. And seven and a half years I think I went to more hospitals than movie houses. And then  in 1982, in December, he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a survivor too?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a one, yes. On the-- after the Kristallnacht, he was taken to Dachau. He was  one month in Dachau. And somehow, somehow, he could get out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was German?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was German. He was born in Frankfurt am Main.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to do this interview with us, Magda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I decide? I wanted to write a book, because so many things happened. And it is only a little part of it, because I went through a lot during the Stalin communism also. And then to immigrate to Israel, to learn a new language, to start a new life. Then I immigrated in '68 to America to learn a language, and to learn another culture, and their other lifestyle.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had what to say. But somehow, never could get to it to write this book. It's emotional. Here at least I have spent a few hours to tell my story. But if I write a book, I have to have still more detail, the whole thing, and to spend a lot of time. And it is a emotional, you know? So all week, while I was waiting for this, nothing else was in my mind. We certainly want to thank you, Magda. You are--

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you because it gives me a lot to tell this story and gives me a lot that I learned that I will get a copy. And I can send to my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you. It, it was a pleasure to be with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mark. You can cut. Speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my great, great, great grandfather. He was a rabbi in Hungary in Paks. And this picture was one of my most cherished possessions. All my apartment was ransacked and everything stolen in Budapest. And when I came back, I found this picture down on the shelter. And somebody stabbed it-- you can see it maybe-- because they had so much hate that they can not even see a picture of a rabbi. And I had a feeling that he sacrificed himself instead of me. And I brought that-- up to this time I was calm, and I could hold myself. But then I broke down. I got hysterical when I saw that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are my grandparents-- Reb Fivel Klein and his wife, my grandmother. And they the descendant of the famous Rabbi Prague who we will talk later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cut. Speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father-- Abraham Arnold Klein and his wife, Herzl Isabella. She was a third cousin of Theodor Herzl who dreamed up the State of Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Cut. Speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Imre Emerick Mautner, my first husband, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: In I don't know really. Maybe it's on the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, do you want to tell us about the second picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The second picture is Irene Gyigor, who saved my life and my son's life, and my son as a baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, cut please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Michael. In Hungary he was called Andras. And this picture is taken after the war. And he went through so many fear and so many terrible things that you see on this picture the sadness and the wondering what happened to him. This is Theodor Herzl, who established the Israel state. He dreamed it up. And he was a third cousin of my mother who is [NON-ENGLISH] Herzl.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Irene [NON-ENGLISH] Gyigor. She died in 1984-- '94. --1994, at age 80. She was my guardian angel and my savior, and was an exceptional human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a certificate and a silver medal what Irene got from the Yad Vashem, given to her by the Swiss Consulate, because that time that she got that was still communism, and there was not a connection between Israel and Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Cut. Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- after the war, I adopted Irene as my sister. And we kept our relationship up to her death. I loved her very much. And I miss her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Cut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know their names, Magda? They're brothers? Yeah, but I don't see the picture. They are my brothers in Israel , who are very religious Jews. And it is-- these, they are my two brothers here. [POINTING] And this is the one of their grandchildren. And the grandchild was the bar mitzvah, had the bar mitzvah. And this is my mother with her four daughters. She had five. The fifth took the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cut. Speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Martin Marx, my  late husband, who I married on November 18, 1970. He was vice president in the Leo Baeck Lodge of B'nai B'rith. And he had a business, educational toys and school supplies. He died in 1982. He was a survivor?

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a survivor. He was one month in Dachau after the Kristallnacht. And somehow he could escape from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Dr. Michael Mautner. He is a research chemist. He became a associate professor in the Rockefeller University, where he got a PhD also. He worked for the National Bureau of Standards. And they are his children, his wife [POINTING] Helene, and his children, Ira, Naiomi, and Joel. Ira is now married and has a baby. And he just got his diploma in biochemistry with first honor in New Zealand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cut. Speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Ezra Benjamin Mautner, my great grandchild. He was born in 1994. He is now-- and the second one is coming already. He's the--
